trajectory_summary: The call opened on a highly qualified, self-motivated customer and ended with a booked appointment — a structurally incomplete booking on a warm lead. The rep completed the verify sequence and captured the bill, but left multiple high-value signals (EV load, Dominion rate increase, slate roof, wife on title) unanchored. The appointment format was never resolved cleanly, and the button-up was missing its core confirmation elements.
outcome_dispute: The call produced a booked appointment, but the booking is structurally incomplete: wife on title was not included in the appointment, the format (phone vs in-person) was contradicted twice, the 'is that fair' handshake was skipped, and the no-driving/computer anchor was absent. The sit is at elevated no-show risk not because Michael is reluctant — he is genuinely warm — but because the rep did not lock the appointment with the schema's required confirmation elements.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: My my wife and I are on the title.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Michael confirmed his wife is on the title at 01:09. The rep said 'Perfect' and never returned to her. The appointment was booked for Michael alone. Per the schema, a co-title-holder must be on the appointment — this is a protocol, not an objection to overcome. The booking as structured excludes a decision-maker, which is a predictable no-close risk even if the sit holds. This is the single most consequential gap in the call because it is fixable at the moment of title confirmation and it directly affects whether the closer can close.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. And we also have, two electric cars that we recently purchased, like, one a year ago. And then I just got a, like, a Tesla. So we're basically gonna be, like, increasing our electric consumption over the next, like, couple years and doing a lot more driving.
key_moments.stewart_read: Michael is doing the rep's job for him — he is articulating a forward-looking load increase, naming the utility (Dominion), and connecting it to a cost motive. This is the single richest anchor point in the call. The rep's response was a personal question about Tesla self-parking ('Does it really back up itself, Tesla?'), which is a rapport tangent with no payoff. The strategic read would be that the rep was building human connection before pivoting to the bill anchor — but there is no evidence of the pivot. The reactive read holds: the rep walked past the anchor entirely.

key_moments.quote: the cost of electricity in our neighborhood has gone up, like, a pretty significant amount in the last year with Dominion.
key_moments.stewart_read: Michael explicitly names Dominion rate increases as a driver. This is a distinct flip opportunity separate from the EV load signal — the customer is already framing the utility as the problem. The schema's objection_inversion mechanic applies directly: the rep could have reflected Dominion's rate trajectory back as the cost anchor ('So Dominion's already been raising rates — and you're about to add two EVs on top of that bill. That's the exact scenario where locking in a solar rate makes the most sense'). The rep's response was 'Okay' and a move to the utility company confirm. Capture without flip.

key_moments.quote: sorry. I'm just, like, finishing texting someone.
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep discloses mid-call that he is texting someone else while Michael is providing his bill range. Michael had just delivered his most detailed and cooperative answer of the call (bill range, gas heat context, 10,000 kWh figure). The disclosure signals divided attention at the call's highest-engagement moment. This is not a strategic pause — there is no setup being built. It is inattention made visible.

key_moments.quote: Our issue is that we have a slate roof. So it's ideally, we would want a lot of ground mount solar panels, like, in our backyard and front yard to kind of, avoid doing any kind of structural damage because it's a rock it's basically like a rock roof.
key_moments.stewart_read: Michael raises a genuine installation complexity — slate roof, preference for ground mount — that is closer-tier content. The rep responded with 'Okay' and moved on. The strategic read is that the rep correctly held setter scope by not engaging in a technical debate about slate roof installation. The reactive read is that the rep had nothing to say and went silent. The coaching need is not about slate roof knowledge — it is about verbalizing the handoff. The rep's silence left Michael's concern unacknowledged, which is a trust gap. The schema's universal escape hatch ('That's exactly the kind of question our specialist is built to answer') would have validated the concern and bridged it to the appointment without scope creep.
